common_name: Black and Blue Salvia (Deep Cobalt-Blue Hummingbird Sage with Black Calyces)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Black and Blue spreads by underground rhizomes to form a gradually expanding colony — a desirable quality for naturalizing in large plantings but plant where spreading can be accommodated or managed.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Black and Blue is one of the most spectacular hummingbird plants available for Gulf South gardens — Salvia guaranitica 'Black and Blue' produces large (2-inch), deep cobalt-blue to ultramarine-blue tubular flowers with contrasting jet-black calyces that frame each flower, creating a vivid blue-and-black color combination that is more intensely blue than almost any other commonly grown summer perennial. The flower color is genuinely deep, pure blue — not the blue-purple of most blue flowers — making Black and Blue one of the most useful plants for achieving true blue in warm-climate garden compositions. The black calyces that remain after the flowers drop maintain the ornamental display and create the impression of more flowers than are actually present. Ruby-throated hummingbirds find this salvia irresistible — the large, deep-blue flowers produce abundant nectar and the flower size is ideal for hummingbird bill geometry. The plants grow vigorously to 3–5 feet and spread by underground rhizomes, gradually naturalizing in suitable conditions. In zone 9, Black and Blue is a semi-evergreen perennial that may be damaged by hard freezes but returns from the rhizomes in spring with mulch protection. Cut back to 12 inches in late winter to encourage fresh, vigorous regrowth.

common_name: Santa Barbara Mexican Sage (Compact Velvety Purple Fall-Blooming Salvia)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Santa Barbara requires the same fall bloom trigger as the species — shorter day length in late summer and fall initiates flowering regardless of other conditions; plants in areas with significant artificial night lighting may not bloom as freely.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Santa Barbara is a compact selection of Mexican bush sage (Salvia leucantha) — more suitable for typical garden spaces than the standard species, which can reach 4–5 feet. At 2–3 feet, Santa Barbara produces the same spectacular display of velvety, deep purple to purple-and-white bicolor flower spikes as the species in a more manageable size. Mexican bush sage flowers in response to shortening day length (photoperiod-triggered), blooming from August through December — one of the most valuable late-season ornamentals for Gulf South gardens that provide important late-summer nectar for migrating monarch butterflies. The flower spikes are composed of velvety, fuzzy purple calyces (even in the bicolor 'white flowers' version, the calyces are purple) with white or purple corolla flowers — the texture is unique among commonly grown sages. In zone 9, Santa Barbara grows as a semi-evergreen perennial, returning from the base after hard freezes; it is at its drought-tolerant best once established in lean soil. Excellent as a late-season companion for fall-blooming Salvia coccinea, Gulf muhly grass, and swamp sunflower. Hummingbirds and gulf fritillary butterflies visit the flowers extensively during the fall bloom period.

common_name: Dixie Crimson Clover (Long-Season Gulf South Winter Annual Cover Crop Clover)

planting.note: Direct sow 1/4 inch deep in fall (October–November in zone 9) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; inoculate seeds with Rhizobium trifolii inoculant before planting to ensure effective nitrogen fixation in new planting areas.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Dixie is the preferred crimson clover variety for Gulf South cover crop use — selected specifically for the warmer winter conditions of the Deep South where it outperforms northern varieties by remaining vegetative longer before bolting to flower, providing a longer window of ground cover, weed suppression, and nitrogen fixation. Crimson clover is the most widely used winter annual legume cover crop in the Gulf South, seeded after the main crop harvest in October–November, establishing through the cool months, fixing atmospheric nitrogen through Rhizobium symbiosis, then terminated by mowing, rolling, or light tillage in spring before planting warm-season vegetables. A well-established stand of crimson clover can fix 50–100 pounds of nitrogen per acre — equivalent to a significant fertilizer application — while also improving soil organic matter, preventing winter erosion, and suppressing winter weeds. The 'Dixie' variety's later bolting (compared to 'Autauga' and other early varieties) is its primary advantage in zone 9: it maintains active vegetative growth through February and into March in most years, providing several extra weeks of weed suppression and soil coverage before initiating the red, conical flower heads that signal the end of the vegetative growth period. The showy crimson flower heads that precede termination are also excellent for early-spring pollinators. Inoculate seed with legume inoculant when planting in fields with no history of crimson clover.

common_name: Shenandoah Pawpaw (USDA-Released Large Mild-Flavored Custard Apple Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container-grown or potted bareroot pawpaw trees in spring in moist, well-drained, deep, fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–7.0) soil in full sun to partial shade; plant at least two different pawpaw cultivars for cross-pollination — pawpaw is not reliably self-fertile and needs a different named cultivar as a pollinator for consistent fruit production.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, deep, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade (young trees benefit from shade for the first year); Shenandoah is a USDA-developed pawpaw cultivar (from the USDA breeding program at the National Clonal Germplasm Repository) considered one of the best for fruit quality — producing large fruits (8–18 oz) with cream-yellow to golden flesh, mild, sweet, clean custard flavor with few of the aftertaste notes that some tasters find objectionable in other cultivars, and moderate seed count. Pawpaw (Asimina triloba) is the largest edible fruit native to North America — a tropical-looking deciduous tree that produces banana-mango-vanilla-flavored fruits unlike any other temperate fruit, earning it the names 'poor man's banana' and 'custard apple'. The unique flavor is genuinely difficult to describe to those unfamiliar with it: deeply tropical and custardy, resembling a banana-mango blend with vanilla overtones. At zone 9, pawpaw is at the warm edge of its range — it performs best in zone 9a where winter chilling hours (approximately 400 hours below 45°F) are more reliably met. In the warmer parts of zone 9, reduced chilling can limit fruit production. Young trees require shade for the first year before developing sun tolerance. Cross-pollination with 'Sunflower', 'Susquehanna', or another cultivar is essential for consistent fruit set.

common_name: Red Rubin Holy Basil (Deep Purple-Red Sacred Tulsi Basil for Tea and Ritual Use)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; 'Red Rubin' produces the most intense deep-purple foliage color in full sun — partial shade produces more greenish-purple leaves with less dramatic color, though the plants still grow well.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Red Rubin Holy Basil is a deeply colored cultivar of tulsi (Ocimum tenuiflorum, holy basil) — the sacred plant of Hinduism, considered the most important of the sacred plants (tulsi) present in virtually every Hindu household as a revered manifestation of the goddess Lakshmi. The deep purple-red to burgundy-red foliage of 'Red Rubin' is substantially more ornamental than standard green tulsi, combining the sacred herb's distinctive aromatic qualities with a plant form visually comparable to red basil varieties used in Western herb gardening. The aroma of holy basil is distinctly different from sweet basil (Ocimum basilicum) — a complex, spicy-clove-mint-camphor fragrance with earthy, slightly medicinal undertones rather than the purely sweet-anise of Italian basil. This aroma makes tulsi specifically suited for herbal teas (tulsi tea is a traditional Ayurvedic beverage), aromatherapy, and as a basil flavor component distinct from sweet basil in fusion cooking. In zone 9, Red Rubin Holy Basil grows as a vigorous warm-season annual from spring through fall, potentially self-seeding in sheltered positions. Regular harvest of stem tips prevents bolting and maintains vegetative growth. The ornamental purple foliage and small lavender-pink flowers make it equally valuable as a garden ornamental and as an herb with the distinctive tulsi fragrance.
